author: @tehspamgozehere
text: Blowing on the flat disc has me immediately thinking of the "Tesla Turbine". A series of flat plates, or near-flat. The small imperfections in the surfaces provide just enough friction to spin up, but because they're almost perfectly balanced and light weight they can get up to truly stunning speeds compared to a traditional rotor. Incredible speeds, but relatively low torque. Might be worth looking at if only to learn they exist.

author: @netikras
text: 9:13 "but the simplest <...>" - IDK if that's true. IMO the simplest bearing would be a plastic Kinder egg (the toy container), a metal/plastic/wooden/etc rod perforating it with one end sticking out longer. The whole thing dropped into a container with water (or some even lower viscosity fluid). Fluid bearing. Dead simple to make, does not require anything special (like ring magnets polarized in a specific direction) and is absurdly cheap. Performance is slightly worse than maglev, but then again, you're always saying that top performance is not the goal, if it will cost a fortune in the end. I imagine filling a bathtub with water and dropping a bunch of Kinder eggs into it would be quite cheaper than buying/making ring magnets :)

@murdocks4304 Having a maintenance free bearing would be nice but that was a side track in this setup.  Imagine the coils, like the ones on the left spindle placed on the right side. author: @earthlingthings
text: Off topic, since I don't have a way to share my rocket stove design to you I'll try to describe it here. You need two tubes of chimney flue that fit over each other. The inner tube sits lower, so it grabs the flame, smoke, etc from the burning fuel. The outer tube is offset height wise to grab air from the surroundings. What happens is, this oxygenated air gets superheated as it passes through the sleeve. Since it sits taller than the inner tube, this heated oxygenated air meets the smoke and licks of flame at the top of the inner tube. Hey presto, it ignites any unburnt fumes. And you get a gas flame at this juncture. If you consider flames in the inner flue travel , say, 30cm, and you offset the outer tube 10cm you've got a brilliant rocket stove. Ive been using my design for 10+ years. It's awesome.

replies.author: @earthlingthings
replies.text: You can fill it with scraps , logs, twigs, whatever. To the height of the inner tube. Light it on top. It burns like a candle.

replies.author: @earthlingthings
replies.text: And at the base, lift it off the ground some 8 cm. So air can feed into the inner tube from below. There's an optimum height for this, since you want the core to stay hot and not be affected by wind. Some rocks ought to do. Or , in my case, I leave 1/3 of the inner tube open at the bottom, cut away.

replies.author: @earthlingthings
replies.text: One last clarification. The diameter of the outer tube is a few mm greater than the inner tube. A slither of a gap. The tubes telescope over each other.

I'm not saying I disagree with you though,  There are a lot of Vawt designs that thin out toward the top, and all of the old sea-faring ships had a similar design on their sails: Fatter toward the bottom, and thinner toward the top (which they learned the hard way, after seeing a few magnificent warships get capsized by strong gusts of wind).

author: @ericblenner-hassett3945
text: The only ' issue ' I see is that now you need to update your CAD files to allow for the greatly variable distance the magnetic Bearing will have due to the differences in the magnets potentially used.  Having the serpentine coil section as just a wall and not part of the base, sections of the ring, with ' steps ' to hold the next segment from the outside may help.  Usually when you connect sections of pipe, it is flared bigger and you stuff the new section into the other and that is the principle behind stepped ' washer ' segments.  The base could have the ring that the current serpentine coil gets wound on inner diameter but a slightly larger outer at roughly a few millimeters and the ' extra ' bit outside higher by a few mill to allow for it to add more segments and keep the whole thing round ( any puns in this were unintentional ).  With those specific magnets, measuring after getting the rotor and base made would assist in getting the serpentine centered around the rotating magnets and thus how much of a ' washer ' segment to use.  Thank you for the video, I forgot how simple it can be to get an equivalent of a thrust Bearing with magnets.

author: @kreynolds1123
text: Just noting that an axial flux generator with a magnetic thrust bearing will have a somewhat undesired self limiting effect when current rushes through the coils.  They will start to act as magnetics that create a bit more levitation that increases the airgap and reduces the output. Maybe the effect will be minimal and tolerable but it's there and one should at least think about it when designing. 😊

replies.author: @tonymazza8305
replies.text: Would using bar magnets instead of button magnets (for better field coverage) mitigate this?

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@tonymazza8305 it might not problematic enough to be overly concerned with. Without doing calculations I can't really say how much. I'm only saying it's there if your using maglev bearing with an axial flux. If that's what you want to build, it's good to be aware of it and that you may want to calculate how much it might effect your project. 

On the other hand, If you turn the field coils and rotor magnets so that their fields point radially then when current flows through the coils, they won't add to the axial levitating force from the thrust bearing magnets.
